design_philosophy.core_loop: Select wrestler, engage in fast-paced strike-based combat against progressively difficult opponents in single or multi-player matches with escalating handicap conditions
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Arcade-style progression through increasingly stacked odds (1v1 → 2v1 → 3v1 gauntlet) requiring pattern recognition and execution mastery
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embody a WWF superstar with exaggerated, over-the-top powers (bleeding character objects, transforming limbs, elemental effects) in spectacle-driven wrestling combat
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Aggressively sacrifices wrestling simulation realism for Mortal Kombat-inspired arcade fighting game mechanics: strike-heavy combat, flawless victories, exaggerated physics, and cartoonish special attacks (e.g., Razor Ramon's arm becoming a blade, Doink pulling mallets from thin air). Prioritizes immediate accessibility and spectacle over authentic wrestling grappling

difficulty.continues_system: Arcade-style checkpoint progression through tournament brackets with ability to continue after defeat
difficulty.checkpointing: Two single-player modes: Intercontinental Championship (4 1v1 + 2 handicap 2v1 + 1 handicap 3v1) and WWF Championship (4 handicap 2v1 + 2 handicap 3v1 + WrestleMania Challenge gauntlet). WrestleMania Challenge escalates from 3v1 initial setup
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Choice of 8 wrestler characters with distinct move sets and bleeding attributes; multiplayer cooperative mode offers shared challenge. No selectable difficulty settings documented

progression.unlock_mechanism: Hidden character Adam Bomb available (incomplete in final version). Eight base wrestlers selectable from start
replayability_hooks: Eight distinct wrestler movesets with unique special attacks and bleeding properties encourage character rotation
Separate Intercontinental and WWF Championship tournament paths with different difficulty curves
Cooperative tag team mode provides alternative narrative path through same roster
Multiplayer head-to-head competitive mode for indefinite replay

world_building_techniques: Commentary by Vince McMahon and Jerry 'The King' Lawler during matches grounds action in WWF broadcast aesthetic
Howard Finkel introduction at start screen establishes WrestleMania event framing
Character-specific entrance music (circa-1994 WWF roster themes) reinforces individual wrestler identity
Announcers visible at commentator table maintains broadcast presentation throughout
Character-unique 'bleeding' objects (dumbbells, hearts, etc.) establish visual personality without narrative exposition

boss_design.philosophy: Progressive handicap escalation creates difficulty curve; later opponents in gauntlet function as bosses through numerical advantage rather than special mechanics
notable_bosses: WrestleMania Challenge gauntlet format (3v1 initial, escalating to 1v1 final encounters) serves as final boss run
Individual wrestlers function as opponents rather than boss entities; no documented boss-specific mechanics beyond move set variation

faction_indicators: Eight distinct WWF wrestlers embody era-specific character archetypes (The Undertaker, Bret Hart, Razor Ramon, etc.)
Kayfabe maintained through commentary and entrance music without explicit storyline exposition
danger_signals: Escalating handicap match structures (2v1, 3v1) signal computational/narrative threat escalation
WrestleMania Challenge gauntlet cumulative opponent cycling creates mounting pressure narrative
environmental_storytelling: Announcers' table with Vince McMahon and Jerry Lawler grounds action in broadcast environment
Ring-side setting and WrestleMania event framing through Howard Finkel introduction
Character-specific entrance music and visual effects (bleeding objects) establish personality without dialogue

sources_used: tcrf - WWF WrestleMania: The Arcade Game (SNES) documentation on port limitations, character roster, developer message
wikipedia - WWF WrestleMania: The Arcade Game gameplay modes, character roster, development notes by Mark Turmell, port specifications and comparative feature analysis
